clues.fit_justification: Source question reads 'What is the Spanish word for blue'; answer 'Azul' is a foreign-language word and the color blue is the definition.

clues.clue: In Spanish, the word 'negro' translates to this color in English.
clues.fit_justification: Source question reads 'In Spanish, what is the meaning of the word negro'; answer 'Black' is a color.

clues.clue: In German, the word 'rot' means this color.
clues.fit_justification: Source question reads 'What does the word rot mean in German'; answer 'Red' is a color.

clues.clue: The Italian word 'nero' translates to this color in English.
clues.fit_justification: Source question reads 'What is the Italian word for black'; answer 'Nero' is a foreign-language word for the color black — clue asks for the color, answer is the Italian term.

clues.clue: Originally another word for poppy, 'coquelicot' is a shade of this color.
clues.fit_justification: Source question reads 'Originally another word for poppy, coquelicot is a shade of what'; answer 'Red' is a color, and the source notes coquelicot is a foreign/archaic word for the color.

categories.title: LANGUAGE FAMILIES
categories.theme: Source question asks which language family a named language belongs to; answer is the language family

clues.clue: Hindi, spoken by hundreds of millions across South Asia, belongs to this language family.
clues.fit_justification: Source question reads 'The language Hindi belongs to which language family'; answer is 'Indo-European'.

clues.clue: Dutch, the language of the Netherlands, belongs to this broad language family.
clues.fit_justification: Source question reads 'The language Dutch belongs to which language family'; answer is 'Indo-European'.

clues.clue: Sylheti, spoken primarily in Bangladesh and northeastern India, belongs to this language family.
clues.fit_justification: Source question reads 'The language Sylheti belongs to which language family'; answer is 'Indo-European'.

clues.clue: Assamese, the official language of the Indian state of Assam, belongs to this language family.
clues.fit_justification: Source question reads 'The language Assamese belongs to which language family'; answer is 'Indo-European'.

clues.clue: Unlike Hindi, Dutch, or Sylheti, the Central Asian language Kazakh belongs to this entirely different language family.
clues.fit_justification: Source question reads 'The language Kazakh belongs to which language family'; answer is 'Turkic', distinct from the Indo-European answers in all other clues.

categories.title: WORD DEFINITIONS
categories.theme: Each clue asks for a word that fits a given definition

clues.clue: Defined as 'lacking enthusiasm or determination', this 13-letter word is often mispronounced as 'lacksadaisical'.
clues.fit_justification: Source question literally reads 'Which word is defined as "lacking enthusiasm or determination"?' — the clue asks for a word matching a definition.

clues.clue: Defined as 'shouting together with joy', this rare adjective begins with the prefix 'con-'.
clues.fit_justification: Source question literally reads 'Which word is defined as "shouting together with joy"?' — the clue asks for a word matching a definition.

clues.clue: Defined as 'two vowel sounds joined in one syllable to form one speech sound', as in the 'ou' of 'out'.
clues.fit_justification: Source question literally reads 'Which word is defined as "two vowel sounds joined in one syllable to form one speech sound"?' — the clue asks for a word matching a definition.

clues.clue: Close to fainting or lightheaded, this informal adjective might describe how you feel after spinning too fast.
clues.fit_justification: Source question literally reads 'Which of these adjectives means "close to fainting or lightheaded"?' — the clue asks for a word matching a definition.

clues.clue: Defined as 'a rumbling or gurgling noise in the intestines', this word is itself almost onomatopoeic.
clues.fit_justification: Source question literally reads 'Which word is defined as "a rumbling or gurgling noise in the intestines"?' — the clue asks for a word matching a definition.

categories.title: LANGUAGE FAMILIES & ORIGINS
categories.theme: Clues about which language family a language belongs to, or the linguistic origin of a word

clues.clue: From the Sanskrit word for 'duty', this term refers to the teachings of the Buddha.
clues.fit_justification: Source question literally reads 'From the Sanskrit word for "duty", what word refers to the teachings of the Buddha?' — directly references linguistic/etymological origin.

clues.clue: Mandarin Chinese belongs to this language family, which also includes Tibetan and Burmese.
clues.fit_justification: Source question literally reads 'The language "Mandarin Chinese" belongs to which language family?' — directly asks for a language family.

clues.clue: Marathi, a language spoken in western India, belongs to this vast language family that also includes English and Hindi.
clues.fit_justification: Source question literally reads 'The language "Marathi" belongs to which language family?' — directly asks for a language family.

clues.clue: Moroccan Arabic belongs to this language family, which includes Hausa, Amharic, and Somali.
clues.fit_justification: Source question literally reads 'The language "Moroccan Arabic" belongs to which language family?' — directly asks for a language family.

clues.clue: In phonetics, the 'd' in the word 'door' is an example of this type of consonant sound, produced by briefly stopping airflow.
clues.fit_justification: Source question literally reads 'In phonetics, which of these is an example of a plosive sound?' and the answer is 'The d in "door"' — directly references a phonetic/linguistic classification.

finals.title: WORD ORIGINS
finals.clue: This chemical element takes its name from the Greek word meaning 'stranger', fitting for a gas so unreactive it rarely combines with anything.
finals.fit_justification: Source question literally reads 'What chemical element gets its name from the greek word meaning stranger?'; answer is 'Xenon' — a direct etymology/word-origin question.

finals.title: LANGUAGE & MISUSE
finals.clue: This Sheridan character, who appears in 'The Rivals,' became so notorious for her comic misuse of long English words that her name entered the language as a term for that very error — calling a pineapple 'an allegory on the banks of the Nile,' for instance.
finals.fit_justification: Wait — source_id tapi:622a1c367cc59eab6f9503da is 'New Coke.' Correct source is tapi:622a1c367cc59eab6f9503de. Using correct source: source question reads 'Which character invariably misused english words?' and source answer is 'Mrs Malaprop' — directly about language misuse, giving rise to the word 'malapropism.'

finals.title: OBSOLETE ENGLISH WORDS
finals.clue: Defined as an 'outdated word meaning armpit,' this seven-letter term beginning with O once appeared in standard English dictionaries.
finals.fit_justification: Source question: 'Which word is defined as outdated word meaning armpit?' Source answer: 'Oxter'. Literally defines an archaic English word, ideal for a language-themed final.

finals.title: LATIN PHRASES
finals.clue: This Latin phrase, 'Magna est vis consuetudinis', translates to a sentiment about the power of repeated behavior, later echoed by Aristotle.
finals.answer: Great is the power of habit
finals.fit_justification: Source question asks 'How does the Latin phrase Magna est vis consuetudinis translate into English?' and source answer is 'Great is the power of habit'.

finals.title: WORD ORIGINS
finals.clue: The name of this durable ribbed cotton fabric is derived from a French phrase meaning 'Cord fit for the King', making it one of fashion's most regal etymologies.
finals.fit_justification: Source question literally reads 'What is the name of the durable cotton fabric derived from the French for Cord fit for the King?'; source answer is 'Corduroy', directly citing the French etymological origin.
